data.title: Both OpenAI and Anthropic wanted to continue their dispute before their IPOs.
data.description:
The conflict between OpenAI and Anthropic won't stop, because it can't afford to. Anthropic needs an insecure adversary to justify its security narrative, while OpenAI needs a hypocritical adversary to justify its open narrative. This structure dictates that the other's very existence is its best advertising material, with each attack reinforcing the motivation for the next. Both companies are simultaneously rushing to the IPO window, and this infighting also serves a more practical function: preemptively defining their valuation logic in the eyes of investors. Whoever has the more legitimate narrative will receive a higher valuation. The internal letter that OpenAI's Chief Revenue Officer, Denise Dresser, sent to all employees in early April is actually a product of this logic. Because this internal letter wasn't written for employees. Dresser dedicated an entire section to a point-by-point attack on Anthropic: its brand narrative is built on fear, limitations, and the control of AI by a select few; its flawed computing power strategy has led to product throttling and a decline in user experience; and Anthropic's claimed $30 billion annualized revenue is overstated by approximately $8 billion because it accounts for its revenue sharing with Amazon and Google on a gross basis, while OpenAI uses a net basis for its revenue sharing with Microsoft. It's almost unprecedented in the tech industry for a company's revenue officer to dedicate a section in an internal memo to dissecting a competitor's accounting practices. The $8 billion accounting allegations reached reporters at Bloomberg and The Verge within a day. The week before, OpenAI sent a separate memo to investors, stating that Anthropic was operating on a smaller growth trajectory. The core of this $8 billion accusation is an accounting term. When Anthropic distributes Claude through AWS and Google Cloud, the full amount paid by customers to the cloud platforms is included in Anthropic's revenue, while the commission shared with the platforms is treated as a cost—this is the gross method. OpenAI, on the other hand, uses the net method for its share with Microsoft, only recording the portion it actually receives. Both methods are legal under US GAAP. Anthropic argues that it acts as a principal in the transaction, with the cloud platforms merely serving as distribution channels. OpenAI counters that the net method is the standard that publicly traded companies are required to follow. Both sides have valid points, but the key is not just the logic, but the outcome. If recalculated on a net basis, Anthropic's comparable revenue dropped from 30 billion to 22 billion, just short of OpenAI's self-reported 25 billion. During the window of opportunity when both companies are rushing to IPO, the company with the larger revenue figure directly determines its ranking in investors' minds. Anthropic's revenue was 9 billion at the end of 2025, but jumped to 30 billion by March 2026, a growth rate more than three times that of OpenAI. If this momentum takes root in investors' minds, OpenAI's 850 billion valuation will face a critical question: Why is it twice as expensive as Anthropic, yet its growth rate is three times slower?

Therefore, Dresser's $8 billion accusation has only one purpose: to subconsciously instill in investors the idea that Anthropic's figures cannot be viewed at face value.

But there's an ironic aspect to this attack: Dresser also heavily promoted OpenAI's new partnership with Amazon in the same letter. If OpenAI distributes itself extensively through Bedrock in the future, it will also face the same gross and net issues.

The whip used to punish others today may fall on oneself tomorrow.

The accounting dispute is only the most easily quantifiable part of this letter. What's more worthy of analysis is Dresser's qualitative judgment on the essence of the Anthropic brand.

Fear or Responsibility? "Their narrative is built on fear, restriction, and the idea that a small elite should control AI." Most reports treat this as an insult, but it's more than that. Dresser is repricing the product. She's trying to get investors to see Anthropic's security brand premium as an artificially created scarcity, a packaged differentiation. On April 7th, Anthropic announced its most powerful model ever, Claude Mythos Preview, while simultaneously announcing that it wouldn't be publicly released. Instead, a restricted access program, Project Glasswing, allows 12 core partners to use Mythos to scan for and fix zero-day vulnerabilities in critical software. Anthropic claims that Mythos has discovered thousands of high-risk vulnerabilities in every major operating system and browser in the past few weeks, including a FreeBSD remote code execution vulnerability that has existed for 17 years. A powerful model, too robust to be publicly released and restricted to a few elite organizations, is used to protect the world's cybersecurity—this is the pinnacle of Anthropic's security narrative. Although researchers have pointed out that some security companies can reproduce some of the vulnerabilities discovered by Mythos using older, cheaper, publicly available models, Anthropic has packaged this discovery and exploitation of the vulnerability chain as a watershed moment, using it as a reason to lock in its product release schedule. In OpenAI's narrative, Glasswing is the perfect example of a select few controlling AI. You have a powerful tool, you don't let most people use it, you claim it's for security, but the objective effect is that only those you trust can access this capability. In Anthropic's narrative, Glasswing is the ultimate proof of responsible AI development. We are powerful enough to find vulnerabilities others can't, and restrained enough not to release this capability directly to the market. Both narratives serve their respective IPO valuation models. Anthropic needs investors to believe that the security brand premium is sustainable. OpenAI needs investors to believe that scale and computing power are the ultimate moat. Who fired the first shot? This is a seemingly simple question that actually has no answer. Because there is too much evidence, and each piece can be interpreted by both sides as the other making the first move. If you look at it from Dario Amodei's perspective, the starting point is the collapse of governance within OpenAI. In 2017, he witnessed Musk's ruthless layoffs; in 2018, after Altman took over, he promised to limit the power of Brockman and Sutskevi, while simultaneously making contradictory promises to the latter two. In 2020, Altman accused the Amodei siblings of secretly passing negative feedback to the board, but denied saying this after being confronted. These experiences shaped a deep distrust. If you look at it from Altman's perspective, the starting point is the way Dario left. In late 2020, Dario took nearly ten core colleagues to found Anthropic. In his farewell memo, he categorized AI companies into market-oriented and public interest-oriented types. In this brand positioning manifesto, OpenAI was preemptively placed in the lower ethical category. Every public statement Anthropic made since then, from billboards in San Francisco to Super Bowl ads, reinforced this categorization. The entanglement and feud between these two individuals and their respective companies can be traced back to the founding of OpenAI in 2015. At each step, someone made a seemingly justifiable choice, accumulating into an irreversible rift. Who struck first is not important; what matters is why this hostility persists, and why each conflict reinforces itself. The answer lies in the brand structure. Once Anthropic positions itself as the righteous party that has abandoned its security ideals, OpenAI automatically becomes the problematic party that has been abandoned. Once OpenAI characterizes Anthropic as an elitist fear peddler, every cautious action Anthropic takes becomes evidence of this accusation. The two brands are mutually exclusive, meaning the other's very existence is the best advertising material for its own brand. Anthropic needs an insecure adversary to justify its security narrative. OpenAI needs a hypocritical adversary to justify its open narrative. This is a Nash equilibrium; neither side has an incentive to stop attacking because a truce would damage their respective brands more than continuing the fight. Every upgrade in 2026 perfectly illustrates this structure. In February, Anthropic ran four ads during the Super Bowl, each beginning with a screen-filling barrage of the words "betrayal," "deception," "breach of trust," and "infringement," mocking OpenAI's decision to insert ads into ChatGPT. The ads ended with the tagline, "Ads are entering AI, but not Claude." Altman responded with a 420-word post on X, first saying the ads were funny and he laughed, then using terms like dishonesty, Anthropic-esque double entendre, and selling expensive products to the wealthy. New York University marketing professor Scott Galloway commented that when you're a market leader, you should never mention your competitors' names; Hertz never mentions Avis, and Coca-Cola never mentions Pepsi. Altman's response itself was a sign of weakness. At the New Delhi AI Summit on February 19, Modi invited thirteen tech leaders on stage to hold hands for a photo. Everyone did, except for Altman and Dario, who stood next to each other. The two raised their fists, but there was no physical contact. This image went viral on social media. Altman later said he didn't know what happened, and Dario did not comment publicly. Later that month, a dispute erupted at the Pentagon. Anthropic refused to remove two exceptions from its contract and was subsequently suspended by federal agencies. Hours later, OpenAI announced a contract with the Pentagon. In March, a judge ruled that the Pentagon's actions constituted unconstitutional retaliation, and Claude briefly rose to the top of the App Store charts. In the first week of April, Anthropic released Mythos and Glasswing. In the second week of April, two OpenAI memos were leaked in quick succession. Each round of attacks occurred at crucial junctures, such as closing funding rounds, product launches, or IPO preparations. So, in this back-and-forth, mutually attacking rhythm, who is reaping the benefits? The winners aren't in the ring. Dresser wrote a seemingly mild statement at the end of the letter: "Customers will benefit from the competition." But on the eve of the IPO, customers aren't the winners of this war of words; investors are. Beyond investors, there's a group of quieter beneficiaries. Amazon is the biggest winner. In her letter, Dresser bluntly stated that the partnership with Microsoft limited OpenAI's ability to reach customers, while praising the need for the Amazon Bedrock channel. This implies that OpenAI is positioning Amazon as its second marriage, but Anthropic's first marriage was also built on AWS. Amazon has invested $50 billion in OpenAI and is also one of Anthropic's largest cloud partners. Regardless of who wins, Amazon is in the game. Even more interestingly, Dresser's core evidence accusing Anthropic of inflated revenue involves Amazon itself. If this accounting dispute is ultimately decided by the SEC during the IPO review, Amazon, as the other party in the transaction, will hold the most crucial facts. It is evidence in the judge's hands, and also the sponsor of both players. The second winner is Google. When OpenAI and Anthropic define each other using each other as a point of reference, Google is not within this binary narrative framework. Gemini 3 surpassed ChatGPT 5.1 in benchmarks by the end of 2025, but because media and investor attention was drawn to the dramatic conflict between OpenAI and Anthropic, Google gained narrative freedom, allowing it to quietly advance its products without needing to imbue every action with ethical implications of safety or openness. The third winner is the regulator. OpenAI co-founder Greg Brockman personally donated $25 million to the pro-Trump MAGA committee, while a political action committee he co-founded with a16z raised over $125 million to support unified federal regulation and oppose independent state legislation. Anthropic donated $20 million to Public First Action, an organization supporting stronger AI regulation. Both companies used political donations to buy into their preferred regulatory environments. Anthropic also hired Ballard Partners, a lobbying firm closely linked to the Trump administration, after the Pentagon dispute. This firm, which rejected military contracts based on security principles, turned around and bought a ticket to the White House in Washington. The fiercer the war of words, the greater the need for lobbying in Washington for both companies, and the stronger their bargaining power with regulators. The ultimate winner is the IPO underwriter. The conflict between the two companies saved investors the trouble of understanding the technological differences. Do you believe in safety first? Buy Anthropic. Do you believe in scale first? Buy OpenAI. For the sell-side, this is the easiest sales pitch. You don't need to make investors understand complex technological differences; you just need to ask them one question: Which side are you on? A business competition encoded as a family feud no longer needs data to convince investors; it needs a stance. All these winners have one thing in common: their interests don't depend on who wins, but on whether the battle continues. The more the two companies fight each other, the higher Amazon's channel value, the heavier the regulatory leverage, and the easier it is for underwriters to sell their story. And neither party seems to intend to stop, because if they did, their respective valuation stories would lose the most dramatic half.

data.title: Why did Hermes take over from OpenClaw in just two months?
data.description:
Entering April 2026, OpenClaw (commonly known as "lobster"), which had only been popular for two months, faced its challenger.OpenClaw  (commonly known as "lobster") Hermes Agent has topped the GitHub Trending charts for several consecutive weeks, garnering a whopping 22,000 stars. How popular is it? Even Anthropic is copying it. On April 10th, Teknium, founder of Nous Research, criticized Anthropic, saying it was "copying" Hermes' ability to automatically determine task completion and proactively remind users. The community narrative is therefore very unified, believing that Hermes, with its self-evolving agent, automatic memory management, and user modeling system, has comprehensively surpassed its predecessor, OpenClaw, in terms of technology, redefining the direction of open-source agents. However, if we set aside these grand narratives and truly compare the two side-by-side, we'll find that they are functionally identical in far more ways than they differ. For example, both have scheduled tasks. Hermes supports human-readable formats and standard cron expressions, with each task running in an isolated session. OpenClaw also supports at, every, and cron scheduling types, with tasks directly persisted to a local JSON file, ensuring they are not lost upon restart. For example, sub-agent delegation exists on both sides. Hermes' `delegate_task` supports single tasks and up to three parallel sub-tasks, with completely isolated sub-agent environments that return only a summary upon completion. OpenClaw's sub-agent mechanism also supports this background isolation execution and result feedback, and even allows configuration of nesting depth. Browser automation, TTS (text-to-speech), Vision visual capabilities, image generation, and voice interaction are also available on both sides. On the gateway side, message integration with over 20 platforms such as Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, and Signal is also unsurprisingly available on both sides.

Checking off each item on the list reveals that the functions of the two are almost completely overlapping. The so-called "absolute crushing" in the function list simply does not exist.

So the question is, since the functions are the same, why is Hermes so popular? How much of the "self-evolution," "automatic memory," and "user modeling" touted in the community are actually differences in underlying structure?

Skills that Grow on Their Own

After going through the default configurations on both sides, the only hardcore difference you can find is that Hermes has implemented a closed loop of automatic evolution for Skills.

A Skill is essentially a workflow knowledge unit for an Agent. To put it simply, it's a Markdown file that specifically tells the Agent what steps to take when encountering a certain type of task, what tools to use along the way, and how to recover if things go wrong.

Hermes has artificially split the lifecycle of a Skill in two.

Hermes has artificially split the lifecycle of a Skill in two.

The next time a similar task is encountered, it will automatically scan the index. This loading process is done in four progressive layers, like finding information in a library. It first looks at the catalog cards (Tier 0), only stuffing the names and descriptions into the system prompts, which take up about 3,000 tokens. Once it's in the right direction, it then goes to the bookshelves layer by layer to retrieve the books and expand the complete content.

The next time a similar task is encountered, it will automatically scan the index. This loading process is done in four progressive layers, like finding information in a library. It first looks at the catalog cards (Tier 0), only stuffing the names and descriptions into the system prompts, which take up about 3,000 tokens. Once it's in the right direction, it goes to the bookshelves layer by layer to retrieve the books and expand the complete content.

Building this set 

First released by Hermes on February 25, 2026, it was touted as "An Agent that grows with you." With its aggressive approach of proactive memory, automatic evolution, and forcibly making decisions for users, it quickly reached 57,200 stars.

However, in the v0.7 resilience update released on April 3, it actually quietly took a step back.

Hermes introduced an abstract interface, downgrading Honcho, the only high-level memory backend that was originally hard-coded into the system, to be on par with six newly integrated third-party services, including MEM0 and ByteRover. Instead, the most basic pure file-based full-text search became the default fallback solution. A system that claimed to decide everything for you proactively stripped away the complexities, returning the power of memory selection to the user. This was a strategic concession by the pioneer after encountering genuine complaints from the community. Hermes retracted somewhat, feeling that the current rule system couldn't handle all complex scenarios, and some choices didn't need to be forced upon the user. On the other hand, OpenClaw took a completely opposite approach, focusing on catching up and strengthening its capabilities. In just a few days, it made two significant moves. On April 5th, it released Dreaming, similar to Claude Auto Dream, for offline memory organization, refining, scoring, and promoting existing short-term transactional documents into persistent MEMORY.md entries offline. In its April 10th update, it introduced Active Memory, running a dedicated memory agent directly before the main response. This proactive approach, with its large model acting as the referee, is more granular and intelligent than Hermes' fixed 15-round fine-tuning. This shows that both Anthropic and OpenClaw are moving towards the "making decisions for you" model. Hermes simply bet the earliest and most aggressively. In two months, it firmly established its dominance in the narrative of fully automated agents and captured users' minds. While OpenClaw desperately tried to catch up, supplementing its active memory mechanism with more advanced sub-agents, Hermes, having already established its position, had the luxury of caution and room to retreat. It's not betting on how perfect today's system will be, but rather setting up a game in advance. It calculated that as long as the underlying model's context can cross that passing grade, the safety margins of today's hastily constructed rules will rise accordingly, snapshots can be updated in real time, and hard-coded conditions can be confidently entrusted to the large model for judgment. Those seemingly conservative default layers will one day completely devour today's seemingly sophisticated interface layers. First, secure the ecosystem niche, then wait for technological upgrades. By the time the qualitative change occurs, Hermes will already be standing at the finish line, holding real users, a skill ecosystem, and a foundation of memory. In the cutthroat world of agents, whoever secures their position and makes their product usable with a safety net when their technology is just barely adequate is often far more effective than simply having a technological lead. This is true for OpenClaw, and it's true for Hermes today.

data.title: An employee laid off by OpenAI turned a $225 million investment into $5.5 billion.
data.description:
In less than 12 months, a former OpenAI employee turned a $225 million investment into $5.5 billion, betting on an area almost entirely unknown to Wall Street. In an after-hours trading session in April 2026, Bloom Energy's stock price surged 15% within hours. This company, often described as a clean energy solutions provider, primarily manufactures solid oxide fuel cells to power large factories and data centers. On the surface, it seems to have no connection to neural networks or large language models. But at that moment, the market value of Leopold Aschenbrenner's holdings climbed from $875 million to nearly $2 billion. Leopold Aschenbrenner was born in Berlin, Germany in 2001 to parents who were both doctors. At the age of 15, he left Germany for the United States, and at 19, he graduated first in his class from Columbia University, where he delivered the commencement address. He then joined OpenAI's "Superalignment" team to research how to prevent AI from going out of control. In April 2024, he was fired for pointing out numerous security vulnerabilities in the company and expressing concerns about the theft of model weights. Upon leaving, he refused to sign a multi-million dollar agreement with a no-comment clause, leaving behind the words, "Freedom is priceless." After leaving, instead of quietly seeking employment, he locked himself in his room and spent months drawing the straight line in his mind onto paper. What's on Page 165? In June 2024, he published a 165-page book titled "Situational Awareness: The Decade Ahead." The core reasoning tool isn't a complex model, but rather "orders of magnitude" (OOMs), where each OOM improvement equals a tenfold increase. He plotted the trends of the past five years on a chart: GPT-2, released in 2019, had model capabilities roughly equivalent to a preschool child; GPT-4, in 2023, was equivalent to a bright high school student. In four years, this cognitive leap is equivalent to going from kindergarten to high school, driven by an increase in computing power of approximately 3 to 4 OOM, and an algorithmic efficiency improvement of roughly the same order of magnitude. Leopold believes that the evolution of AI is based on a three-in-one growth formula. Physical computing power increases by approximately 0.5 OOM per year, and algorithm efficiency increases by approximately 0.5 OOM per year; these are just two verified trends. The third trend he adds is "unhobbling." Early versions of GPT could only predict text and couldn't perform tasks. However, the model's capabilities were constantly artificially limited: no tool calls, no long-term memory, and no framework for autonomous action. The so-called "unbinding" means removing the shackles that imprisoned the model's intelligence. In Leopold's view, GPT-4 was like a genius locked in a dark room, possessing vast knowledge but unable to effectively interact with the real world. These limitations weren't a matter of computing power, but a design problem. Once unbound, the same model could do completely different things, just like the same engine on a tractor versus a race car—the output is on a completely different scale. His conclusion is that by the end of 2027, effective computing power will increase by approximately 100,000 times, at which point models will possess the ability to independently conduct research and iterate experiments, i.e., AGI. “Models just want to learn,” he wrote. “The bigger you make them, the more they learn. As long as this line doesn’t bend, 2027 is there.” From Models to Electricity This reasoning has sparked considerable discussion in the AI ​​community. As models become more powerful, the demand for computing power grows exponentially. Computing power requires chips, and chips need to be plugged in. Leopold calculated that by 2028, a leading AI training cluster will consume approximately 10 gigawatts of electricity. What does 10 gigawatts mean? It's equivalent to the output of a large nuclear power plant, or five times the total installed capacity of the Hoover Dam. Leopold points out in his paper that the US power infrastructure has been stagnant for decades, with almost no large-scale expansion. He said, "US power production has barely grown for decades. Now we're really in for a ride." This is why Bloom Energy is at the top of his portfolio. Its fuel cells can be deployed directly next to data centers, generating electricity on-site using natural gas, completely bypassing the inefficient public power grid. In April 2026, Bloom signed a 2.8 gigawatt direct power agreement with Oracle to power Oracle's new data centers. Following the announcement, the stock price rose 15% in after-hours trading that day.

Holdings Around AI

Situational Awareness LP, officially launched in September 2024, raised $225 million in initial funding from Silicon Valley investors including Patrick Collison, John Collison (co-founder of Stripe), and Nat Friedman.

Its initial funding did not flow to glamorous software companies, but rather to heavy fuel cells, rusty transformer quotas, and electrical infrastructure considered outdated.

Its initial funding did not flow to glamorous software companies, but rather to heavy fuel cells, rusty transformer quotas, and electrical infrastructure considered outdated.

His portfolio is highly concentrated: Bloom Energy (fuel cells, on-site power supply) is his largest holding, followed by CoreWeave (AI computing power leasing) at approximately $700 million, Core Scientific (approximately 10% equity, Bitcoin mining transitioning to AI data center hosting), Intel call options, and a short position in Indian software outsourcing giant Infosys. He believes AI programming tools will massively replace manual outsourcing, and each of these bets represents a different facet of his prediction that AGI will arrive in 2027. For him, investing is not about finding pleasure in the ticking of numbers, but about expressing an ultimate confirmation of the path of world evolution. Twelve months later, its publicly disclosed holdings surged from $225 million to $5.5 billion. "If we're right, what then?" he predicted. He predicted that once the threshold of AGI is crossed, an intelligence explosion will occur instantly. Hundreds of millions of AGIs will simultaneously conduct AI research, compressing algorithmic advancements that take humans ten years to achieve into a single year. The journey from human-level to far surpassing human capabilities is not a long process, but a very short window of opportunity. Whoever masters superintelligence will gain a decisive economic and military advantage. Around 2027-2028, the United States will be forced to fully intervene, forming a national-level AGI project, comparable in scale to the Manhattan Project. In January 2025, Stargate's $500 billion infrastructure plan was announced, aiming to build an unprecedented AI cluster across the United States. CoreWeave, in which he has a significant stake, is a core partner in this plan. In Dwarkesh's podcast, the host asked this 24-year-old, who already possesses $5.5 billion and may even have seen through the script of the next decade, what he ultimately wants besides this wealth. Leopold didn't mention luxury goods or social status; he said, "I meant the galaxies."

data.title: With HSBC and securities firms entering the market simultaneously, Hong Kong's stablecoin has entered its next phase.
data.description:
Recently, Hong Kong has released several key signals regarding stablecoins. From regulators clarifying the licensing schedule, to market institutions initiating the second batch of license applications, and the banking system beginning to promote stablecoin integration into local payment networks—these are all routine policy-driven actions when viewed individually. However, when viewed within the same timeframe, the direction is quite clear: stablecoins are transitioning from a "pilot tool" to a "system building phase." On April 13th, Hong Kong Financial Secretary Paul Chan's statement set the tone for Hong Kong's stablecoin strategy: "small steps, quick progress." This signifies that regulators are seeking a delicate balance between embracing innovation and mitigating risks. The first batch of licenses will be issued in a "small number," and stringent requirements will be imposed on applicant institutions—they must have "real-world application scenarios." This is clearly to ensure that stablecoin projects can truly take root, rather than being castles in the air. The regulatory authorities will adopt a "pilot first, then rollout" strategy, considering issuing a second batch of licenses only after the first batch of projects have matured and accumulated experience. This caution precisely reflects the robustness of Hong Kong's financial system. Nature and Positioning: Secretary for Financial Services Paul Chan clearly stated that stablecoins are essentially "payment instruments," not "investment instruments." This definition sets clear boundaries for the future development of stablecoins in Hong Kong and also indicates that their regulatory focus will revolve around areas such as payment clearing and anti-money laundering. Under this regulatory framework, the structure of market participants is also undergoing significant changes. According to disclosures, Futu Securities and OSL Group, among other institutions, have participated in the second batch of stablecoin license applications. This signal clearly indicates that the issuance and circulation of stablecoins is shifting from being primarily driven by native crypto projects to being dominated by licensed financial institutions and compliant platforms. This means that stablecoins are no longer just liquidity tools within the on-chain ecosystem, but are being incorporated into the infrastructure of the traditional financial system. More significantly, this signifies the advancement of application scenarios. HSBC is pushing forward with the integration of its stablecoin into local payment systems, including PayMe and the HSBC HK App, supporting peer-to-peer transfers and merchant payments. This move signifies that the use cases for stablecoins are expanding from transactions and settlements to retail payments. Once the relevant system completes its closed loop, its functional positioning will undergo a fundamental transformation: from "a category of digital assets" to "a form of digital currency that can directly participate in daily economic activities." At the currency level, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) has also released more flexible policy space. On April 11, HKMA Deputy Chief Executive Chan Wai-man revealed that stablecoin issuers can freely choose the currency to issue. Although the Hong Kong market will initially launch a Hong Kong dollar stablecoin, the issuance of other currencies is permitted in the future. This reveals a significant amount of information: The Hong Kong dollar stablecoin will become a pioneer in the Hong Kong stablecoin market, consolidating its position as an international financial center and providing a regulated digital channel for Hong Kong dollars for local and international users.

  • Multi-currency outlook: Hong Kong's policy flexibility opens the door for the future issuance of stablecoins based on more major international currencies, highlighting its diversification and openness as an international financial center. ...span inline-dir="ltr" ql-global="true">Multi-currency outlook: Hong Kong's policy flexibility opens the door By considering the above multiple dimensions, it can be seen that what Hong Kong is currently promoting is not a single policy pilot, but a relatively complete institutional design: At the entry point, the licensing mechanism controls the participating entities. At the entity level, licensed financial institutions will be introduced as core participants. At the application level, priority will be given to promoting the implementation of payment scenarios. At the institutional boundaries, space will be reserved for multiple currencies, especially the RMB. The core objective of this system is not simply to expand the size of the digital asset market, but rather to integrate stablecoins into the financial infrastructure system under controllable risks. In the current complex and volatile international environment, Paul Chan emphasized that Hong Kong needs to remain highly vigilant and achieve "proactive defense" by enhancing the competitiveness and global influence of its financial markets. This statement refers not only to traditional financial risks, but also to institutional competition within the emerging digital asset system. Stablecoins are currently at the crossroads of this competition. The 24/7, cross-market monitoring system also means that its development path will always be under strong regulation and high transparency. This further demonstrates that Hong Kong's promotion of stablecoins is not a passive choice driven by the market, but a proactive deployment based on financial strategic considerations. Against this backdrop, the significance of stablecoins is shifting. They are no longer merely liquidity tools in the crypto market, nor simply technical means of cross-border fund transfers, but are gradually evolving into a financial medium with "institutional attributes" under different regulatory systems. In other words, the future use of stablecoins will no longer depend on technological feasibility, but rather on the regulatory system you operate under.

data.title: Unpacking $184 billion: A comprehensive analysis of Tether's first audit.
data.description:
Introduction

On March 24, 2026, Tether, the issuer of USDT, the world's largest stablecoin, officially announced that it had signed a contract with one of the Big Four accounting firms (the specific firm was not disclosed) to initiate its first full independent financial statement audit in nearly 12 years since its establishment.

As a digital asset company, Tether's main business is issuing USDT, a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar. According to the official announcement in March 2026, Tether's market capitalization exceeded $184 billion, with more than 550 million users worldwide. Regarding asset compliance, Tether had never previously undergone a full independent financial statement audit; its quarterly reserve certificate reports had been issued by BDO Italia, a subsidiary of BDO, the world's fifth-largest accounting firm, since 2022.

After years of avoiding audits, Tether has finally initiated its own audit process, an event that has attracted significant attention from all parties in the market. This article will further analyze the multiple factors that prompted Tether to initiate its audit, as well as the impact of this first audit on Tether and the entire crypto industry, in order to provide some insights for market participants. I. What does Tether's first audit cover? 1.1 The difference between attestation and auditing Tether's launch this time is not a new round of attestation to reserves, but rather its first complete and independent financial statement audit, as officially stated. From a professional perspective, although both attestation and auditing belong to third-party attestation, there are significant differences between the two. Attestation is a limited attestation service where an independent accounting firm issues a point-in-time statement on a specific date, confirming that the stablecoin issuer's reserve assets are at least equal to the total circulating supply of tokens. A financial audit is a comprehensive examination of a company's entire financial system, covering the balance sheet, income statement, cash flow statement, internal control framework, governance agreements, and compliance with accounting standards. In terms of focus, certification typically revolves around specific matters, primarily confirming whether there is a correspondence between the issuer's disclosed reserve assets and the liabilities of circulating tokens; auditing, on the other hand, targets a more complete financial system, focusing not only on assets and liabilities themselves but also extending to financial statements, internal controls, reporting processes, and related governance arrangements. Simply put, the difference lies in the fact that certification addresses the question of "whether a specific disclosure is valid," while auditing further answers the question of "whether the company's overall financial information is more reliable." Currently, obtaining certification reports from independent third parties remains a common practice for stablecoin issuers to self-disclose; therefore, Tether's shift from certification to a comprehensive audit is not merely an upgrade in disclosure format, but rather a pioneering step in advancing the transparency requirements for stablecoin issuers to a more complete financial audit framework. 1.2 Main Contents of this Audit According to the official announcement, Tether's complete independent financial statement audit covers not only the company's existing reserve assets but also information on the company's systems, financial reports, and internal controls. Although Tether did not disclose further specific audit details in its official announcement, the complexity and workload of the audit are considerable, especially considering the reserve assets alone. According to the reserve asset certification report for the fourth quarter of 2025 issued by BDO, as of December 31, 2025, Tether's total assets were approximately US$192.9 billion, and its total liabilities were approximately US$186.5 billion. In terms of asset allocation, Tether directly holds over $122 billion in U.S. Treasury bonds, a record high; its direct and indirect Treasury bond exposure, including overnight reverse repurchase agreements, exceeds $141 billion, making Tether one of the world's largest holders of U.S. Treasury bonds. Its reserves also include precious metals, Bitcoin, secured loans, and a small amount of corporate bonds. Furthermore, Tether's proprietary investment portfolio through its global investment fund in areas such as artificial intelligence, energy, and fintech exceeds $20 billion, but this portion of funds originates from excess profits and is strictly segregated from USDT reserves. For the auditors, Tether's assets represent a composite reserve structure spanning traditional financial assets and digital assets. The auditors not only need to verify the existence of these assets but also need to further audit the ownership, valuation, liquidity, and matching relationship between different assets and token liabilities.

II. Why did Tether initiate this audit? 2.1 Global Regulatory Framework is Tightening Simultaneously From the current market situation, the continuous tightening of the global cryptocurrency regulatory framework is a key factor prompting Tether to initiate a comprehensive audit as soon as possible. In the United States, President Trump signed the GENIUS Act in July 2025, establishing a unified regulatory framework for payment stablecoins at the federal level for the first time. This law requires issuers to fully back their circulating payment stablecoins with eligible reserve assets and to publicly disclose the reserve composition monthly. For issuers with a circulating market capitalization exceeding $50 billion and not subject to the periodic reporting obligations of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, it also requires the preparation of annual financial statements in accordance with U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP), which must be audited by a registered accounting firm according to applicable auditing standards and then publicly submitted to regulatory agencies. In Europe, the EU's Crypto Asset Markets Regulation (MiCA) rules on Asset Reference Tokens (ART) and Electronic Money Tokens (EMTs) directly related to stablecoins have been in effect since June 30, 2024. The European Banking Authority (EBSA) has subsequently further developed a direct supervisory framework for ART and EMTs, with accompanying rules imposing more detailed requirements on the liquidity of reserve assets and the proportion held in credit institutions; for "significant stablecoins," the minimum holding ratio can be increased to 60%. In Asia, Hong Kong's Stablecoin Ordinance came into effect on August 1, 2025. Institutions issuing fiat-backed stablecoins in Hong Kong or Hong Kong dollar-pegged stablecoins overseas must apply for a license from the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA). On April 10, 2026, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) officially issued the first two stablecoin issuer licenses: PointPoint Financial Technology Limited (license number FRS01, a joint venture established by Standard Chartered Bank (Hong Kong), Hong Kong Telecom, and Animoca Brands) and The Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation Limited (license number FRS02), marking the formal entry of Hong Kong's stablecoin regulation into the licensing and implementation phase. A series of regulatory developments in different regions globally indicate that the compliance threshold for stablecoins is narrowing simultaneously, and the external requirements faced by leading issuers have changed. In the past, proof of reserves could, to some extent, satisfy the market's basic concern about whether issuers could adequately support their reserves; however, as the regulatory framework becomes increasingly institutionalized, market focus has gradually shifted from the reserve coverage relationship itself to the issuer's financial transparency, internal controls, governance capabilities, and whether it can accept higher standards of continuous external verification. For Tether, a complete audit has become a "top priority," and one of the key tasks of appointing Simon McWilliams as CFO was to drive the company towards a complete financial audit. If Tether does not proactively take the step of auditing, it faces the risk of being systematically excluded from the mainstream financial system. 2.2 Historical Compliance Controversies Affect Market Trust Since the launch of USDT in 2014, questions about whether "each USDT is fully backed by a 1:1 dollar-denominated asset" have never ceased. In April 2019, the New York State Office of the Attorney General (OAG) launched an investigation and applied for an injunction against Tether and its affiliated exchange Bitfinex's operator, iFinex. The investigation found that from mid-2017, Tether struggled to maintain normal banking relationships and, at times, failed to back USDT in circulation at a 1:1 ratio with sufficient dollar reserves in its own bank accounts. Furthermore, OAG disclosed that Bitfinex suffered approximately $850 million in losses due to its partnership with Panamanian entity Crypto Capital Corp., and that Tether's reserves were used to cover this shortfall—both companies were owned and controlled by the same small group of individuals, creating a significant conflict of interest. In February 2021, the two parties reached a settlement: Bitfinex and Tether were required to cease all trading activities with New York users, pay an $18.5 million fine, and publicly disclose the composition of assets guaranteeing Tether on a quarterly basis, including loans or receivables with related entities. In October of the same year, the U.S. Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) imposed a $41 million civil fine on Tether, finding that it made false statements regarding reserve adequacy between June 2016 and February 2019. According to the CFTC announcement, in the 26-month sample period from 2016 to 2018, Tether held sufficient fiat currency reserves on only 27.6% of the days. This means that for most of that time window, Tether's claimed full dollar guarantee was not actually fulfilled. Although Tether began issuing quarterly reserve verification reports through BDO Italia after the settlement, gradually disclosing its asset composition to the market, such verification is still significantly different from a complete independent financial statement audit. "Snapshot" verification reports alone are far from sufficient to repair the deep-seated trust gap in the market regarding Tether. 2.3 Financing Obstacles Force Increased Transparency Tether is one of the world's most profitable crypto companies. According to its disclosures, its full-year profit in 2025 exceeded $10 billion, with excess reserves reaching $6.3 billion and holdings of $141 billion in US Treasury bonds. However, high profitability did not automatically translate into recognition from the capital market. Looking at the subsequent financing progress, the market is no longer truly focused on profitability itself, but rather on whether these profits, reserves, and asset allocations can withstand higher standards of external verification. In November 2025, S&P Global Ratings downgraded Tether's stablecoin stability assessment from 4 (limited) to the lowest level of 5 (weak). This assessment system measures a stablecoin's ability to maintain its peg to fiat currencies, with scores ranging from 1 (very strong) to 5 (weak). S&P cited the continued increase in USDT's exposure to high-risk assets, including Bitcoin, gold, secured loans, and corporate bonds, along with limited disclosure of related information. Furthermore, according to Bloomberg, Tether sought a large-scale private placement in September 2025 at a valuation of approximately $500 billion, planning to offer about 3% of its equity and raise up to $20 billion. Cantor Fitzgerald, a US financial services company, served as the lead advisor. If the financing was completed at this valuation, Tether would rank among the world's most valuable private companies, alongside SpaceX and OpenAI. However, Tether's fundraising has not gone smoothly. In February 2026, according to the Financial Times, the proposed fundraising amount had been drastically reduced from $15 billion to $20 billion to approximately $5 billion due to investor concerns about valuation and transaction size. Investors' core concerns focused on two aspects: first, Tether had never undergone a full independent audit, resulting in insufficient financial transparency; and second, the regulatory environment remained uncertain. When a company with annual profits exceeding $10 billion cannot complete fundraising at the expected valuation, the market is effectively repricing the "lack of auditing." In this context, auditing is not only a compliance requirement but also a prerequisite for Tether's entry into mainstream capital markets. III. What impact will this audit of Tether have? 3.1 For Other Stablecoin Issuers: Industry Transparency Standards Are Being Forced to Rise Tether, as the largest stablecoin issuer by market capitalization, has taken the lead in launching the Big Four audits, objectively setting a higher standard of transparency for the industry. Previously, stablecoin issuers generally used the limited verification method of proof of reserves. Tether's move may drive the industry's minimum compliance benchmark to shift from proof to audit. For small and medium-sized issuers that are still at the proof level, they may face greater disadvantages in institutional cooperation and regulatory access. The stablecoin industry may be about to usher in a round of elimination based on compliance capabilities. 3.2 For Institutional Investors: Audits Open a Compliance Channel for Crypto Asset Allocation For a long time, institutional investors such as pension funds, sovereign wealth funds, and large asset management companies have maintained a cautious approach to the crypto market. One of the core reasons is the lack of financial transparency of the underlying assets, which has been tested by recognized standards. USDT, as the most widely used medium of exchange and settlement tool in the crypto market, is almost impossible for institutional funds to completely avoid exposure to once they enter the market. Given that Tether has not yet undergone a full audit, investment strategies involving USDT lack sufficient risk control basis, and many institutions' related plans are often shelved during the internal approval stage of compliance departments. If Tether's reserves ultimately pass the Big Four audits and receive unqualified opinions, USDT will receive the endorsement of the world's most stringent financial assessment standards for the first time. Institutional investors' compliance departments will then have formal documentation to submit to regulatory agencies, and the compliance threshold for institutional funds entering the crypto market will be lowered accordingly. 3.3 For Exchanges and DeFi Protocols: The Liquidity Landscape May Be Reshaped USDT plays a core role in the crypto market's liquidity infrastructure. According to Tether's Q4 2025 report, as of December 31, 2025, the circulating supply of USDT was approximately $186.5 billion. Among major global crypto exchanges, USDT boasts the strongest trading pair liquidity. Market makers provide quotes using USDT, users use USDT to enter and exit various assets, and numerous lending and liquidity pools in DeFi protocols use USDT as underlying collateral. It can be said that USDT is deeply embedded in every aspect of the crypto trading system; once its liquidity is compromised, the impact will quickly spread throughout the entire market. Therefore, the audit results have a two-way impact on exchanges and DeFi protocols. If the audit is successful, USDT's position in the compliant market will be further consolidated. Especially in licensed exchanges in the US and Asia, USDT verified by the Big Four audits will more easily gain regulatory compliance approval. However, if the audit exposes significant reserve issues, exchanges may be forced to reduce their USDT exposure and increase alternative trading pairs, and DeFi protocols will also need to reassess the security of assets backed by USDT. 3.4 For Accounting Firms: How the Stablecoin Audit Precedent Will Change the Audit Industry The significance of Tether's initiation of its first complete independent financial statement audit for accounting firms lies not only in securing a large crypto client business, but also in the fact that such audit projects, which have long been in a highly controversial and complex field, are beginning to be included in the scope of business that mainstream auditing firms can formally enter and continue to operate in. The standards set by the Big Four auditing firms cover dimensions such as internal control, related-party transactions, valuation methods, and going concern, far exceeding the proof-of-concept reporting framework currently commonly used in the stablecoin industry. Once Tether completes the audit and obtains a formal opinion, it will become the first major stablecoin issuer to undergo a comprehensive financial audit. For regulators, the audit results will provide a benchmark for stablecoin disclosure standards; for other issuers, market and investor expectations for transparency will be raised overall, and maintaining the existing minimum disclosure levels may no longer be sufficient. Meanwhile, the practices of the Big Four crypto audit firms are themselves accumulating methodologies for the industry. Once leading firms begin to gain experience in this area, stablecoin audits may shift from individual projects to new types of professional services. The methodology developed in the Tether audit has the potential to become a reference paradigm for future crypto company audits, driving the establishment of the entire industry's audit infrastructure from scratch. IV. Conclusion For Tether, the signing of the audit contract is only the starting point; when the final report will be released, what reserve details it will reveal, and whether the audit opinion is unqualified are still challenges to overcome. If Tether passes the audit smoothly, it will put a temporary end to its long-standing reserve controversy and provide a crucial foundation of trust for the mainstreaming process of the entire crypto industry. Regardless of the outcome, this step has already changed the game for the industry. This audit is not just examining Tether's reserve assets, but also whether the entire crypto asset industry is ready to have its commitments put to the test.

data.title: tether.wallet: Tether's most important strategic transformation in 12 years
data.description:
Written by: JSAI@Jinse Finance On April 14, 2026, Tether, the world's largest stablecoin issuer, officially launched its first consumer-grade product for ordinary users—tether.wallet, positioned as "The People's Wallet." This marks the first time in twelve years that the company has moved from a purely backend infrastructure role to a user-facing one. I. What is tether.wallet? Core Functions and Asset Support tether.wallet is a self-custodied crypto wallet that allows users to hold and send USDT, USAT, XAUT, and BTC across multiple blockchains. Tether has deliberately simplified its asset support. The wallet only supports four assets: USDT (the world's most widely used stablecoin), USAT (Tether's compliant stablecoin for the US market), XAUT (gold-backed token), and BTC. There are no altcoins, no DeFi tokens, and no NFTs. Behind this "minimalist asset list" lies a clear product philosophy: a payment tool and a store of value, not a speculative platform. In terms of network support, USDT and XAUT initially support Ethereum, Polygon, Plasma, and Arbitrum; USAT only supports Ethereum; and Bitcoin supports both the mainnet and the Lightning Network. Tether stated that it will continue to expand the supported blockchain networks. Three Major Experience Innovations: Human-readable addresses, eliminating alphanumeric errors. Users can send funds using human-readable formats such as [email protected], completely eliminating the historical problem of irreversible errors caused by long alphanumeric wallet addresses. This design is similar to the logic of email addresses, bringing the cryptocurrency transfer experience closer to the cognitive comfort zone of ordinary people. Fees are paid directly with transferred assets, without the need for Gas tokens. The wallet removes common friction points: users no longer need to hold separate Gas tokens to pay transaction fees; fees are deducted directly from the transferred assets. This is significant for ordinary users—previously, someone holding only USDT needed to purchase ETH or POL as Gas fees before initiating a transfer, a barrier that deterred many non-professional users. Fully self-custodied, the keys are always in the user's hands. The wallet is fully self-custodied. Private keys and mnemonic phrases are generated and stored on the user's device; no transactions are signed on the server, and Tether never holds user funds. Furthermore, the wallet supports securely backing up private keys to the cloud, a feature that has attracted attention from some in the crypto community—critics point out that similar recovery tools previously launched by Ledger have been controversial due to cloud backups, and it is currently unclear whether Tether will allow users to completely disable cloud backups.

Underlying Technology: The Dual Engines of WDK and QVAC

tether.wallet is not an independent product, but rather a consumer portal within Tether's broader technological strategy.

WDK (Wallet Development Kit) is its technological foundation. Tether open-sourced WDK in October 2025—a modular, multi-chain self-custodial wallet building tool designed for developers, institutions, and even sovereign nations, aiming to support humans, connected devices, and AI agents in securely holding and exchanging value on open networks. WDK's first public application is Rumble Wallet, launched in January 2026, connecting crypto payments to 80 million users on the Rumble platform; tether.wallet is the flagship consumer product built on the same foundation.

QVAC is Tether's self-developed localized AI computing platform. QVAC enables wallets to run AI functions locally on user devices, enabling intelligent tasks such as transaction management and financial insights, while protecting user privacy by not uploading data to the cloud.

QVAC is Tether's self-developed localized AI computing platform. QVAC allows wallets to run AI functions locally on user devices, enabling intelligent tasks such as transaction management and financial insights, while not uploading data to the cloud.

This architecture circumvents the cloud-based data processing model commonly used by large technology platforms, aligning perfectly with the current market trend of prioritizing privacy.

II. Why is Tether launching tether.wallet now?

The answer to this question can be interpreted from three dimensions: strategy, business, and regulation.

Strategic Level: Bridging the "Last Mile," Upgrading from Infrastructure to Platform

For over twelve years, Tether has been the underlying layer of the digital asset economy—providing liquidity, settlement, and payment capabilities in over 160 countries. Users have never directly interacted with Tether products. tether.wallet changes all of that.

This is a textbook example of vertical integration. Imagine a company that owns the world's largest water pipe network but has never sold water directly to users—all end-user sales rely on intermediaries (exchanges, payment platforms). tether.wallet is like Tether installing its own branded faucet in users' homes for the first time.

As of March 2026, Tether's technology is used by over 570 million people worldwide, with tens of millions of new wallets added every quarter.

Prior to this, none of these 570 million people directly used Tether's own products. This meant that while Tether had a massive user base, it had no control over end-user behavior. If downstream exchanges or payment platforms encountered problems, Tether's network effect would be at risk of collapse. From a business perspective: Building a data flywheel and expanding business boundaries. Having products directly facing users means that Tether will accumulate first-party user behavior data for the first time. This data can support future product iterations, risk management optimization, and even provide insight reports to enterprise clients—a business value that cannot be achieved simply as back-end infrastructure. Meanwhile, this announcement is part of a series of aggressive moves by Tether in 2026: in March, it brought in KPMG to conduct its first comprehensive financial audit of its $185 billion USDT reserves, advancing USAT's compliance position in the US market; on April 9th, it released the QVAC AI development kit; and on April 14th, it launched tether.wallet. This entire series of moves points in the same direction: in an increasingly regulatory environment, while building institutional credibility, it locks in end-users through consumer products.

Regulatory Perspective: Seizing Strategic Advantage Before the GENIUS Act Takes Effect

Tether's 2026 strategy consists of two lines: KPMG audit to establish institutional credibility, USAT to establish a US regulatory position, and tether.wallet to directly push products to 570 million users.

The coordinated intention of these three is to complete the transformation from "gray infrastructure" to "compliant financial platform" before the formal implementation of the US GENIUS Act and other stablecoin regulatory frameworks. Once the regulatory framework is established, Tether, with its mature compliance system, ample audit records, and large direct user base, will have a significant advantage in terms of entry barriers.

III. The Profound Impact of tether.wallet on the Industry For the wallet market: A new player with a market capitalization of 570 million enters the fray For existing mainstream wallets such as MetaMask and Phantom, the emergence of tether.wallet signifies a fundamental change in the competitive landscape. Previously, these wallets' biggest competitive advantage was user habits and multi-asset support; however, Tether brings its twelve years of accumulated brand trust and the inherent use cases of USDT. More importantly, WDK, the platform behind tether.wallet, is already a proven open platform—Rumble Wallet is its successful commercial application. This means that Tether may continue to license WDK through white-label agreements, allowing more partners to build wallet products within the Tether ecosystem, forming a wallet ecosystem matrix where Tether controls the underlying protocol. For the stablecoin market: User stickiness shifts from platforms to issuers. Previously, users used USDT because they used Binance, OKX, or other platforms, not because they identified with the Tether brand. tether.wallet changes this dependency. Once users establish their [email protected] identity and usage habits within tether.wallet, some of USDT user stickiness will migrate from exchanges to Tether itself. This is a clear pressure signal for competitors like USDC—Circle currently lacks a substantial direct consumer wallet product.

On AI and the Machine Economy: A Human-Machine Shared Payment Infrastructure

Tether CEO Ardoino described his ambition for this wallet: "Tether aims to make digital infrastructure more accessible, serving tens of billions of future users." In the definition of WDK, "users" include not only humans, but also AI agents and autonomous machines. Tether explicitly positions WDK as "a financial infrastructure used by future humans, connected devices, and AI agents in high-speed transactions," designed to support trillions of self-custodial wallets.

This is a highly forward-looking strategic positioning. As the AI ​​agent economy evolves, autonomous payments between machines will become a real need, and self-custodial wallets with native stablecoin support and local AI capabilities will become the infrastructure for this scenario.

On the Industry Competitive Landscape: Blurring the Boundaries Between Issuers and Distributors

The deepest industry significance of the tether.wallet launch is that it breaks down the long-standing role division between "issuers and distributors" in the cryptocurrency industry.

Previously, Tether issued its own stablecoins, exchanges distributed them, and wallets handled them – each with its own role. Now, Tether chooses to directly reach users, effectively playing the triple role of issuer, distributor, and user interface provider. This will force other stablecoin issuers to rethink their distribution strategies and may also trigger vigilance and backlash from existing wallet platforms and exchanges. IV. Hidden Concerns Beyond the ideal narrative, any product has risk factors that require continuous monitoring. The tension between cloud backup and self-custody. tether.wallet allows users to back up their private keys to the cloud, a design that creates a potential tension between convenience and security. True self-custody requires complete user control of the keys, while cloud backup introduces third-party risks. It is currently unclear whether users can completely disable this feature. Centralization concerns regarding human-readable addresses. The [email protected] address format greatly improves the user experience, but it also means that this naming system is centrally operated by Tether. If Tether encounters problems at the corporate level, whether these addresses can continue to resolve normally remains an open question. The dual challenges of regulatory compliance: Launching a consumer wallet product means Tether will directly face stricter consumer protection regulations, a completely different compliance challenge from its previous role as merely a business-to-business infrastructure provider. KYC/AML obligations, user data protection regulations, etc., will all become new compliance costs. Conclusion: The launch of tether.wallet represents Tether's most important strategic transformation in twelve years. It signifies that an infrastructure company that has long operated behind the scenes has chosen to proactively step into the limelight during a crucial window of opportunity when regulatory scrutiny is becoming clearer and the competitive landscape is still uncertain. Regardless of future user growth figures, this move itself has sent a clear signal to the entire industry: the battlefield for stablecoins is extending from the protocol layer to the user end.

data.title: When people oppose AI, what exactly are they opposing?
data.description:
Shan Ouba, Golden Finance

In the spring of 2026, the gunshots in San Francisco's Pacific Heights community shattered a widely accepted assumption: opposition to artificial intelligence may no longer be limited to online condemnation and protests behind screens.

When the flames of Molotov cocktails pierced the night sky, when bullets shattered windows and doors, pointing directly at the residence of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman, we were forced to confront a harsh reality: in the shadow of the rapid iteration of technological revolution, humanity's fear of AI has been distorted into a weapon of harm.

I. 48 Hours, Two Terrifying Attacks

At 2:30 AM on April 11, 2026, in the quiet Pacific Heights neighborhood of San Francisco, a Molotov cocktail suddenly pierced the darkness, crashing heavily against the exterior wall of Sam Altman's residence. Flames briefly erupted, causing only minor damage to the exterior wall and no injuries, but it was enough to shake the entire tech world. Reportedly, three masked suspects quickly got into a black Honda Civic and fled the scene.

Just 48 hours later, panic struck again. At 1:15 a.m. on April 13, at the same location, a car slowly came to a stop, followed by a gunshot that shattered the silence, shattering windows. Fortunately, no one was injured in the attack. Police quickly intervened and arrested two suspects on the spot—25-year-old Amanda Tom and 23-year-old Muhamad Tarik Hussein—who were taken into custody for manslaughter. The FBI has officially launched an investigation, and the case is likely to escalate to terrorism-related charges. Subsequently, an anti-AI organization called "Pause AI" publicly claimed responsibility for both attacks. This is not a Hollywood suspense script, but a real violent incident that occurred to key figures in the tech world in the spring of 2026. II. From Peaceful Petitions to Violent Attacks: The Uncontrolled Escalation of the Anti-AI Movement If we rewind two years, the anti-AI movement remained within the realm of rational protests and peaceful petitions, characterized by restraint and expression of demands. In September 2024, the "AI Security Network" was officially established, its core activities focusing on policy lobbying, public petitions, and exposing industry irregularities, attempting to promote the implementation of AI regulation through legal channels. That same year, hundreds of anti-AI activists gathered outside OpenAI's headquarters, holding signs that read "Stop AI R&D," calmly and firmly conveying their demands to the government and businesses, calling for the establishment of a comprehensive AI regulatory system. The turning point came in 2025, when the anti-AI movement completely shifted, with moderate demands gradually being replaced by radical actions. In August of that year, over 500 protesters besieged OpenAI headquarters, causing chaos and resulting in the arrest of three people for disturbing public order. In November, Google's Oregon data center was deliberately vandalized by anti-AI activists, causing a two-hour power outage and direct economic losses of up to $2 million. This marked the first time anti-AI actions caused substantial property damage and signified the movement's radicalization. Entering 2026, the anti-AI movement accelerated further, with increasingly pronounced violent tendencies. In January, over 120,000 people signed a petition demanding the White House urgently suspend the GPT-5 training program, fearing its potential uncontrollable risks; in February, a large-scale sit-in demonstration erupted outside the Anthropic offices in New York, with 12 protesters arrested for refusing to disperse; in March, Nvidia's data center in California was vandalized again, with losses reaching $5 million, and two suspects arrested; in April, anti-AI activists targeted Sam Altman, a key figure in the AI ​​industry, using Molotov cocktails and bullets to push the protests to a violent peak. From petitions to deliberate vandalism, from property attacks to threats of violence, the escalating trajectory of the anti-AI movement bears a striking resemblance to the Luddite movement in history—in 1811, British textile workers, fearing machines would take their jobs, smashed textile equipment, using extreme methods to resist technological change. History doesn't simply repeat itself, but it always resonates with similar rhythms throughout the ages, providing a historical footnote to the current AI controversy.

III. Six Anxiety Behind Anti-AI Sentiment

To understand the out-of-control nature of the anti-AI movement, we must first understand the deep-seated fears behind it. These fears are not unfounded, but rather the real impact of technological change on human society, our living conditions, and our self-worth.

1. Job Displacement

This is the most direct and widespread fear. Data from 2025 shows that approximately 3.8 million people in the United States have already lost their jobs due to AI applications, including many white-collar positions once considered "stable and secure," such as customer service, translation, basic programming, and data analysis. These jobs are being replaced in large numbers by efficient AI systems.

McKinsey predicts that by 2030, approximately 45% of white-collar jobs globally will be directly impacted by AI, either being replaced or forced to transform. A former claims adjuster who worked at an insurance company for 15 years admitted in a November 2025 interview: “I did claims review for 15 years. Last year, out of 80 employees in my department, only 5 were left to handle ‘abnormal cases’ that AI couldn’t solve. The remaining 75 were all replaced by AI systems. We didn’t even have much buffer time.” For companies, adopting AI can reduce average costs by 60% and significantly increase shareholder returns. However, for ordinary employees, this means reduced income and a sharp increase in the risk of unemployment—the increase in efficiency has not translated into a corresponding increase in employment. This is the cruelest reality behind technological change. 2. Loss of Control Humans are inherently afraid of things that are “incomprehensible and uncontrollable.” No matter how complex traditional technologies are, humans can grasp their core principles and intervene, adjust, or even stop their operation at any time. However, the core of AI technology is "black box decision-making," whose operating logic and judgment criteria are difficult for humans to fully interpret. It may even develop autonomous decision-making capabilities during the learning process, exceeding human expectations and control. As UC Berkeley professor Stuart Russell stated, "The most terrifying misconception humans have about AI is not that it will rebel against humans, but that it will perfectly execute human errors—when we haven't considered all the risks, AI's 'absolute obedience' could have devastating consequences." 3. Increased Social Inequality Technological revolutions should bring universal benefits, but the distribution of AI dividends exhibits severe polarization. Estimates from 2025 indicate that of the total value created by AI, top AI company shareholders take approximately 45%, core AI researchers and engineers divide about 25%, while ordinary workers receive only about 15%, and many unemployed people replaced by AI don't even receive any dividends. A protester from the "Pause AI" organization angrily stated during a demonstration in February 2026: "AI company CEOs and shareholders are making a fortune, earning billions a year, while we lose our jobs and livelihoods because of AI. This isn't technological progress; it's blatant plunder." 4. The Disappearance of Human Uniqueness Art, writing, thinking, and creation—abilities once considered "exclusively human"—are now being broken down one by one by AI. Midjourney-generated artwork won international awards, GPT-5 passed top-level literature tests, and Devin can independently complete complex programming projects—when AI is better at creating, better at thinking, and even better at understanding human emotions and needs than humans, many people begin to wonder: what is the unique value of human existence?

5. Privacy Leaks

The popularization of AI technology has also brought about an unprecedented privacy crisis. Currently, 85% of major cities worldwide have deployed AI facial recognition systems, and 90% of internet user data is collected and analyzed by AI companies. AI's prediction accuracy for personal behavior has reached as high as 78%. Our travel routes, consumption habits, chat logs, and interests are all being quietly captured and interpreted by AI. Humans seem to be living in a transparent world without privacy, and our sense of security is constantly being eroded. 6. Doomsday Panic The media's penchant for exaggerating "AI out of control, human extinction" doomsday scenarios has exacerbated public panic. However, according to industry experts, the probability of these extreme scenarios occurring is extremely low: mass unemployment (85%, already occurred), deepfake abuse (75%, already occurred), AI weaponization (45%, expected in 5-10 years), complete AI out of control (5-15%, expected in 10-30 years), and human extinction (less than 1%). Unfortunately, the media often amplifies low-probability doomsday risks while ignoring the already occurring and widespread real-world harm.

IV. AI Controversies from the Perspective of Celebrities

Faced with the opportunities and risks brought by AI, celebrities from around the world have diverged in their opinions, forming three distinct camps, each holding firm to their positions and engaging in heated debates.

The Opposition: Firmly Call for a Halt, Be Vigilant Against Risks

This camp's voice is the loudest, with its core demand being to suspend AI research and development to prevent potential risks.

In December 2025, Elon Musk publicly stated, "AI is the greatest threat to human civilization, and the consequences would be unimaginable if it is not controlled in time." Apple co-founder Steve Wozniak also spoke out in January 2026, saying, "The development speed of AI has exceeded human control, and it must be paused immediately to reassess its risks." Yuval Noah Harari, author of *Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind*, warned in September 2025, "AI may completely end the human era and reshape the entire ecology and order of the Earth." Most thought-provoking is the statement by Jeffrey Hinton, known as the "Godfather of AI," who resolutely resigned from his position at Google in June 2025 and publicly confessed, "I regret participating in AI research and development. I realize that the technology I created may cause irreparable harm to humanity." Supporters: Embrace Change, Uphold Progress. Supporters firmly believe that AI... It is an inevitable trend of human progress, and fear should not be a reason to hinder technological development. Following the April 2026 attack, Sam Altman responded: "People's fear of AI is reasonable, I completely understand, but we cannot stop progressing because of fear. Historically, every technological revolution has been accompanied by skepticism and fear, but ultimately it has driven the prosperity and development of human society." Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg stated in January 2026: "AI is not a threat, but a tool to liberate human creativity. It can help us get rid of tedious repetitive work and focus on more valuable creations." Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang stated bluntly in December 2025: "AI is the core of the new industrial revolution. It will reshape the global industrial landscape and bring unprecedented development opportunities." AI expert Andrew Ng's view is more rational: "The AI ​​threat theory has been exaggerated by the media. In fact, AI will not replace humans; what will truly be replaced are those who are unwilling to learn and refuse to adapt to AI." a16z founder Marc Andreessen firmly stated: "AI..." It will save the world; it can solve major problems facing humanity such as climate change and disease prevention. Opposing AI is essentially opposing human progress. Neutral Faction: Balancing Innovation and Security Neutral factions attempt to find a balance between "promoting innovation" and "preventing risks," emphasizing the importance of regulation. Bill Gates stated, "AI brings tremendous development opportunities, but also harbors risks that cannot be ignored. The key is not 'whether or not to develop AI,' but how to guide its healthy development—we need a sound regulatory system that neither stifles innovation nor allows risks to spread." Google CEO Sundar Pichai holds a similar view: "The core of AI development is balance; we must maintain the vitality of innovation while upholding the bottom line of safety, ensuring that AI truly serves humanity." Apple CEO Tim Cook emphasized, "AI should adhere to a 'human-centered' approach; all technological research and development should revolve around human needs and well-being, rather than simply pursuing technological breakthroughs." V. A Mirror of History The anti-AI movement is not the first instance of resistance against new technologies in human history. Looking back, every major technological revolution has been accompanied by fierce opposition, and the patterns of history may offer insights into the current AI controversy. From 1811 to 1816, the famous Luddite movement erupted in Britain. Textile workers, fearing that machines would replace manual labor and take their jobs, smashed textile equipment and violently resisted technological change.
