Ultimately, the government suppressed the movement but also recognized the employment problems brought about by technological change, subsequently enacting a series of labor laws to protect workers' rights—technological development is unstoppable, but humanity can reduce its harm through institutional design. In the 1830s, with the rise of railway technology, anti-railway movements also broke out. People worried that trains would damage farmland, frighten livestock, and even believed that "high-speed movement would harm human health," leading to widespread opposition to railway construction. However, railways, with their efficiency and convenience, gradually became widespread, becoming a core driving force of the Industrial Revolution, and the anti-railway movement dissipated. From the 1950s to the present, the anti-nuclear movement has been ongoing, with opponents fearing the devastating consequences of nuclear leaks and nuclear war. This movement has achieved some success; global nuclear energy development is now strictly regulated, and nuclear safety systems are constantly improving. Similarly, since the 1990s, the anti-GMO movement has never ceased, with Europe even completely banning GMO crops, reflecting humanity's cautious attitude towards the risks of new technologies. Today, the anti-AI movement, which began in 2024, continues to ferment, and its outcome remains to be seen. But history has already shown a pattern: in the early stages of a new technology, it will inevitably encounter fierce opposition; in the middle stages, governments and society will intervene to regulate and standardize technological development; in the later stages, as the technology becomes more widespread and people gradually adapt, opposition will gradually weaken. However, it's worth noting that every technological revolution produces both "beneficiaries" and "victims." Some gain opportunities from technological progress, while others lose their livelihoods due to technological change. The core issue has never been "whether or not to develop technology," but rather "how to fairly distribute the benefits of technology" and how to protect those harmed by technology. This is precisely the core demand of the anti-AI movement and a challenge we must confront today. VI. Conclusion The bullet holes in Sam Altman's window are not a threat to one person, but a warning to the entire era. Those who threw Molotov cocktails weren't all crazy. Some were truck drivers who lost their jobs, graduates who couldn't see a future, and parents afraid their children would be replaced by AI. The fear is real, and the demands deserve to be heard, but violence is not the answer. The real answer lies not in the bullets, but in the system, in distribution, and in compensation for those "left behind." History has repeatedly proven that technology itself is neither good nor evil. When AI can do most things that humans can do, or even do them better, what is the value of humanity?

data.title: The "PayPal Mafia" of the AI ​​Era: From Interns to Billionaires
data.description:
Written by: Azuma

Yesterday, a feature interview with Hyperliquid founder Jeff Yan by Colossos magazine went viral online.

In the interview, Jeff Yan revealed a little-known story—during his junior year at Harvard, he participated in the first internship program of quantitative trading giant Hudson River Trading (HRT), with a total of 10 interns selected. Apart from Jeff Yan, who chose the cryptocurrency track, many of his fellow interns have now become prominent figures in the AI ​​field, including Alexandr Wang, head of Meta AI's business line; Jesse Zhang, founder and CEO of Decagon; and Scott Wu, founder and CEO of Cognition.

Jesse Zhang shared a group photo of the interns from that year

According to Scott Wu's supplementary disclosure, the HRT internship program was not the starting point of their friendship. Many of them had already met through the Olympiad competitions in high school (Jeff Yan, Scott Wu, and many others had won gold medals). That small circle also included many other prominent names, including, but not limited to, Johnny Ho, co-founder and CSO of Perplexity; Demi Guo, co-founder and CEO of Pika; Steven Hao, Alexandr Wang's former partner at Scale AI…

During the growth phase of Jeff Yan and Scott Wu, figures like Elon Musk and Peter Thiel represented the "PayPal Mafia"…

"Gangs" have already made their mark in the business world, and people are starting to look for the next similar special network. Jeff Yan and his circle of friends had discussed this topic before, and 19-year-old Alexandr Wang famously asked his friends, "Why can't it be us?" Ten years later, Alexandr Wang's bold statement seems to be coming true. Leveraging AI, this group of young people on the banks of the Hudson River are stirring up the times in their own way. Alexandr Wang: Zuckerberg's AI Brain Alexandr Wang is perhaps the most well-known person in this small circle. Born in Los Alamos, New Mexico in 1997. He is a descendant of Chinese immigrants; his parents were physicists at Los Alamos National Laboratory—the secret laboratory where the first atomic bomb of the United States was developed during World War II. Alexandr Wang has been passionate about mathematics and programming since childhood. He qualified for the Mathematical Olympiad in 2013, the US Physics Team in 2014, and reached the finals of the US Computer Olympiad in 2012 and 2013. In 2015, Alexandr Wang dropped out of MIT and founded Scale AI the following year, annotating data used to train AI in computer vision and audio transcription. Riding the wave of the AI ​​boom, Scale AI's valuation soared, reaching $7.3 billion in 2021, and Alexandr Wang, who owns 15% of the company, saw his net worth surpass $1 billion. In June 2025, Meta, which had clearly fallen behind in the AI ​​race, spent $14.3 billion to acquire a 49% stake in Scale AI. Zuckerberg's condition was that Alexandr Wang, the then 28-year-old key figure in Scale AI, must join Meta. Alexandr Wang subsequently joined Meta, taking charge of Meta's AI development team, Meta Superintelligence Labs (MSL). On the night of April 8th, Zuckerberg's gamble paid off when MSL officially released its first self-developed AI model, Muse Spark. Muse Spark is a native multimodal inference model that supports tool calls, visual chain thinking, and multi-agent orchestration. This is Meta's most powerful model to date. During training, MSL observed predictable scalability improvements in the model's pre-training, reinforcement learning, and inference phases during testing. Scott Wu: Olympiad Prodigy, Creator of a Billion-Dollar AI Industry

Scott Wu was born in 1997 in Louisiana to a Chinese immigrant background. Growing up, Scott Wu actively participated in programming and mathematics competitions, winning three gold medals in the International Olympiad in Informatics, including first place in 2014.

After graduating from high school, Scott Wu attended Harvard University, but dropped out after two years. While an undergraduate at Harvard, he participated in the 2016 International Collegiate Programming Contest (ICPC) as a member of the school team, which won a gold medal and achieved third place overall.

In 2019, Scott Wu co-founded the social platform Lunchclub as its Chief Technology Officer. In 2023, Scott Wu co-founded Cognition with friends Steven Hao and Walden Yan (both Olympic gold medalists), serving as CEO. In 2024, the Cognition team launched Devin, the world's first autonomous AI software engineer. This product can independently complete code writing, testing, and deployment, and supports the decomposition and collaboration of complex tasks, significantly outperforming GPT-4 in the SWE-bench benchmark test. In May of the same year, Cognition secured $175 million in funding led by Peter Thiel's Founders Fund, valuing the company at $2 billion post-money. In September 2025, Cognition raised another $400 million, pushing its valuation to $10.2 billion. As of early 2026, Cognition's annualized revenue had reached $400 million. Johnny Ho: Net worth $2.1 billion, once interested in acquiring TikTok and Chrome. Like Scott Wu, Johnny Ho, a Harvard graduate, won three gold medals at the International Olympiad in Informatics, including a perfect score in 2012. In August 2022, Johnny Ho co-founded Perplexity with Aravind Srinivas, Andy Konwinski, and Denis Yarats. Perplexity positions itself as an AI search engine company that provides conversational search engine services, displaying citation sources for answers and offering relevant question suggestions. In 2023, Perplexity already had 10 million monthly visits; by April 2024, its monthly active users had reached approximately 15 million. That same year, Perplexity embarked on a frenzied fundraising campaign, raising $5 million in its fourth funding round at the end of the year, reaching a valuation of $9 billion; in July 2025, Perplexity completed another $100 million funding round, raising its valuation to $18 billion. It's worth noting that Perplexity has made several ambitious "David and Goliath" acquisition proposals (with venture capital firms willing to fund them), including a takeover bid for TikTok in early 2025, proposing to merge Perplexity, TikTok's US operations, and a new capital partner into a new entity, and a proposal to Google in August 2025 to acquire its core product, the Chrome browser, for $34.5 billion. According to the latest data from Forbes, Perplexity is now valued at $20 billion, and Johnny Ho's personal wealth has reached $2.1 billion. Jesse Zhang: AI Startup Valued at $4.5 Billion in Three Years

Jesse Zhang was also born in 1997 and grew up in the San Francisco Bay Area. From high school onwards, Jesse Zhang was a typical "competition fanatic"—two-time winner of the American Mathematical Olympiad (MOP), a finalist in the Intel STS, and participated in MIT's RSI research project. After entering Harvard, Jesse Zhang completed four years of university coursework in just three years.

In 2018, Jesse Zhang and friends founded Lowkey, a gaming highlight sharing platform. The project received seed funding from Y Combinator and Series A funding from a16z.

In 2021, Lowkey was acquired by Niantic, the developer of Pokémon GO, for an undisclosed price. In 2023, Jesse Zhang and his partner Ashwin Sreenivas co-founded Decagon, focusing on automating enterprise customer service with AI agents to address the high labor costs and low efficiency of customer service centers. In June 2024, the newly established Decagon quickly secured $35 million in funding, including a $5 million seed round led by a16z and a $30 million Series A round led by Accel. Four months later, Decagon raised another $65 million in Series B funding. In June 2025, it raised $131 million in Series C funding, valuing the company at $1.5 billion. In January 2026, it raised $250 million in Series D funding, with its valuation soaring to $4.5 billion. Along with its rising valuation, Decagon's revenue generation capabilities also increased. By the end of 2025, the company's disclosed annual revenue had exceeded $30 million. Demi Guo: A Post-95s Pioneer in AI Video Generation from Hangzhou Demi Guo was born in Hangzhou, China in 1999. During her childhood, her family moved to Silicon Valley, USA. Demi Guo won a silver medal at the 2015 International Olympiad in Informatics. She graduated from Harvard University with a Bachelor of Science degree in Mathematics and a Master of Science degree in Computer Science. She then dropped out of her PhD program at Stanford University to focus on her entrepreneurial career in generative AI video content creation. In April 2023, Demi Guo co-founded Pika with Chenlin Meng, serving as CEO. Pika focuses on developing AI technology for video generation. Its core products include the Pika 1.0 and Pika 2.0 models, which support the generation of 3D animation, comics, cartoons, and movie-style content, offering features such as video extension, canvas expansion, and element replacement. In terms of funding, Pika completed a $20 million seed round before its official launch; subsequently, in November 2023, it completed a $35 million Series A funding round, led by Lightspeed Venture Partners; and in June 2024, Pika completed an $80 million Series B funding round at a valuation of $470 million, led by Spark Capital, with participation from Greycroft, Lightspeed Venture Partners, and actor Jared Leto, among others. Steven Hao: AI Tech Guru Worth Over $1 Billion

Steven Hao, a graduate of MIT's mathematics department, also won a gold medal at the International Olympiad in Informatics (IOI). He was Alexandr Wang's partner at Scale AI and has now joined Scott Wu's Cognition as CTO. Both companies have been described in detail previously, so they will not be repeated here.

Forbes data shows that Steven Hao, at only 30 years old, is now estimated to have a personal fortune of $1.3 billion.

Epilogue: We May Be Witnessing a New Legend I've considered giving this small circle a new name, similar to the "PayPal Mafia," such as the "Hudson River Mafia," or the broader "Olympia Mafia"... Although the historical context and story trajectory are completely different, they seem to share the same core spirit as the previous generation of the "PayPal Mafia"—behind the camaraderie of Olympia, what truly connects them is a shared pursuit of intellectual density, engineering efficiency, and system refactoring capabilities, and a profound prediction of where the future will begin. A new generation of entrepreneurs has stepped into the limelight. Before them lies the question of "How AI will reshape the world," a question far more challenging than the Olympiad itself. This is their battlefield, and their stage.

data.title: A Brief History of Web3 Airdrops: A Look at Twelve Iconic Anti-Airdrop Projects
data.description:
Once upon a time, airdrops in the cryptocurrency world were exhilarating "get-rich-quick myths." In the era of Uniswap, ENS, and Arbitrum, early users and project teams mutually benefited, and evangelists and builders shared the spoils, forming a brief but real "golden honeymoon."

However, turning the clock back to 2023-2026, the influx of massive capital, the extreme infighting of professional studios, and the insatiable appetite of project teams completely distorted the airdrop landscape.

"Interactive rewards" degenerated into a "cyber harvesting ground," and airdrops transformed from early-stage profits into a systemic reverse harvest.

Retail investors were redefined: free testers, low-cost liquidity providers, and a continuous stream of data producers.

In an environment where rules are opaque and expectations are repeatedly rewritten, what often ends up is not a reward, but rather being wiped out, diluted, or even directly eliminated. This article reviews 12 landmark "anti-airdrop" projects in airdrop history, examining how trust is gradually eroded. 1. Hop Protocol (HOP): Ushering in the "Sybil" Era. ● Anti-airdrop process: HOP, a star cross-chain bridge project, pioneered the chilling "Community Reporting Sybil" mechanism. The rules were extremely enticing: the reporter could take a share of the reported address. It felt like Shang Yang, who practiced collective punishment thousands of years ago, had traveled to Web3. ● Anti-airdrop characteristics: A grassroots mutual harm among the masses. The project team delegated the dirty work of verifying on-chain address associations to users, exploiting human greed to incite infighting within the community, and even directly uploading the list of whistleblowers to GitHub for the entire industry to "reuse." ● Profound Impact: After HOP, combating Sybil became "politically correct" for all token-issuing projects, and on-chain interaction transformed from "experiencing decentralized products" into an extremely cutthroat cat-and-mouse game. While combating Sybil has its necessity, completely shifting the responsibility of verification to the community, and even encouraging mutual harm, severely damaged the community ecosystem.

2. Blast: The Father of the Evil "Points System"

●The Anti-Penalty Process: Blast, with its top-tier Paradigm aura, abandoned the traditional interaction model, requiring users to lock up ETH or stablecoins in exchange for "points." The rules were changed frequently, resulting in huge profits for large holders and top NFT players, while ordinary users, after locking up their tokens for months, saw their token returns even fall short of the interest rates of risk-free financial products during the same period.

●Characteristics of the Anti-Penalty System: Ponzi scheme-like and blind box-like gambling.

●Characteristics of the Anti-Penalty System: Ponzi scheme-like and blind box-like gambling.

... Users are swept up in endless FOMO, becoming free ATMs for project teams' TVL data. ● Profound Impact: Since Blast, "points-based systems" have become the industry standard. The original intention of points systems was to encourage long-term user participation, but frequent rule adjustments and severe imbalances in rewards ultimately led to users losing trust in the projects. Web3 freeloaders became Web2 laborers, and the decentralized spirit that Web3 prided itself on died completely under the calculations of capital.

3. LayerZero (ZRO): The Tipping Point of Trust Collapse

●The Anti-Fraud Process: After 18 months of cross-chain interaction that caused users to burn through huge amounts of gas fees, the project team launched the strictest witch vetting in history on the eve of token issuance, even requiring users to "voluntarily confess" in exchange for a portion of their retained shares, otherwise they would be wiped out. A large number of real active users and small studios were wiped out.

●Anti-Fraud Characteristics: Extremely arrogant "presumption of guilt".

●Anti-Fraud Characteristics: Extremely arrogant "presumption of guilt".

The project team pocketed the exorbitant fees contributed by users, then turned around and treated them like thieves, guarding against and humiliating them. ● Profound Impact: LayerZero destroyed the grand narrative of "multi-chain interaction." While the manipulation of transaction volume by "Witchcraft" (a fraudulent platform) required censorship, the brutal implementation of the "presumption of guilt + confession mechanism" further accelerated the collapse of trust. From then on, Tencent (the company behind LayerZero) became infamous, and "anti-fraud" became a Damocles' sword hanging over the heads of all those who exploit loopholes. Retail investors finally understood: in the face of absolute interpretation, your efforts are worthless.

4. zkSync (ZK): The Complete End of the L2 Interactive Airdrop Era

●The Anti-Fraud Process: As one of the four former L2 kings, zkSync kept the community in suspense for several years. After absorbing hundreds of millions of dollars in gas fees, its airdrop rules revealed a shocking black box: the weight of transaction count and activity level was greatly reduced, and "funds retention over a specific period" was used as the core threshold. This resulted in a large number of long-term interactive users who had accompanied the project's growth receiving nothing, while internal insider trading and new accounts that had hastily deposited money received huge shares. ● Anti-fraud characteristics: Using "activity level" to scam Gas, then using "funds" to kick people out. ● Far-reaching impact: zkSync's extremely ugly practices made the entire market completely despair of L2 airdrops. Controlling witches and volume-boosting groups was necessary, but the opaque rules disheartened genuine early contributors. Subsequent L2 launches faced the predicament of "no one caring," and no retail investors were willing to become free on-chain laborers anymore.

5. Infinex: Public Sale Mechanism Collapse

●The Anti-Fraud Process: As a cross-chain DeFi aggregation platform endorsed by Synthetix founder Kain Warwick, Infinex was once seen as a representative of "orthodoxy" by the community. It induced users to invest significant funds and effort through Patron NFTs and a months-long points program. However, when the public sale began in January 2026, the community was met with an extremely high FDV valuation, an absurd "mandatory one-year lock-up," and a chaotic distribution logic.

6. Linea: The term "black slave" originated with Linea

●Anti-slave process: It took PUA art to an outrageous level: It launched the Galxe Odyssey quest, which lasted for two years and had an absurd number of phases. Users had to answer questions, cross-chain, swap, and mint illiquid garbage NFTs like black slaves, and finally they were forced to cooperate with extremely cumbersome KYC.

●Anti-slave characteristics: An indefinitely prolonged war of attrition.

Forever doing tasks, forever accumulating LXP points, forever being subjected to manipulation and psychological warfare, while the mainnet's token launch remains forever out of reach. ● Profound Impact: Linea turned "completing tasks to get airdrops" into a low-paying, mentally exhausting full-time job. Many users became so exhausted that they quit, marking the complete bankruptcy of the OAT (On-Chain Achievement Token) narrative.

7. Grass: DePIN's Free Generator

●Anti-Penetration Process: As a star in the DePIN track, it encourages users to contribute idle bandwidth by leaving their computers running. Countless people left their computers on 24 hours a day to farm points, even paying out of their own pockets to buy clean overseas IPs. As a result, when the token was issued, the project team kept the vast majority of the shares for themselves or allocated them to VCs. Retail investors worked hard to mine tokens for several months, but the money they got from selling them couldn't even cover the electricity bills and the cost of proxy IPs.

●Anti-Penetration Characteristics: Getting something for nothing.

●Anti-Penetration Characteristics: Getting something for nothing.

... Under the guise of Web3 development, it blatantly exploits the physical resources of Web2 users. ● Profound Impact: Grass's anti-exploitation efforts made the market realize that many so-called DePIN projects are essentially "free software," directly leading to a precipitous drop in retail investor participation in subsequent similar projects. 8. Monad: The L1 Airdrop Terminator ● Anti-exploitation Process: As a highly anticipated high-performance L1 project, Monad attracted long-term testnet interaction from the community. In October 2025, the MON airdrop was launched. Although 230,000 addresses were open for applications, the overall community allocation ratio was only about 3.3%. Many genuine testnet users were wiped out or received only meager shares due to strict Sybil vetting, while KOLs and some early associates received large allocations. ● Characteristics of the anti-freeze strategy: Extremely low allocation and strict vetting after high expectations. The project team attracted a large number of testnet users with a technical narrative, then distributed the tokens to KOLs. ● Far-reaching impact: The Monad incident further lowered the community's expectations for airdrops in new L1 projects. Although it was announced early on that the testnet wouldn't count, this wasn't stopped during the process, and none were received during the TGE, leaving genuine early contributors feeling betrayed. Subsequently, enthusiasm for similar high-performance L1 projects significantly declined, accelerating the shift in the L1 sector from "a hundred flowers blooming" to "cautious observation."

9. Babylon: The Incompatibility and Misapplication of the Bitcoin Ecosystem

●The Anti-Airdrop Process: Attempting to forcibly transplant Ethereum's staking mechanism to the Bitcoin network. During the mainnet event, due to the capacity limitations of the BTC chain and extremely high network congestion, many retail investors paid exorbitant miner fees but still failed to stake, directly resulting in real financial losses. Those users who were lucky enough to successfully stake found, after locking up their tokens for six months, that the airdrop returns were far less than those from trading on exchanges or buying financial products.

● **Characteristics of Anti-Fraud:** Extremely high trial-and-error costs. Forcibly creating FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) sentiment on the BTC chain, which does not support smart contracts, ultimately leads to retail investors being mercilessly burned by exorbitant gas fees. ● **Far-reaching Impact:** It poured extremely cold water on the overheated BTC L2 market. It proved with painful lessons that simply replicating Ethereum's PUA (Personal Aid) model is fundamentally unworkable in the Bitcoin ecosystem. This severely eroded the trust and patience of veteran Bitcoin players in this emerging ecosystem.

10. Backpack: The Backlash of Frenzied Volume Boosting and the Trust Crisis of "Chinese Ponzi Schemes"

●The Anti-Fraud Process: Backpack, which raised $37 million, launched a "trading volume = points" activity, and manipulated the community for two years. On the eve of TGE, a sudden crackdown on strict KYC and the "one device, one IP" black box witch hunt resulted in the wiping of a large number of accounts. The survivors suffered equally badly: a large investor boosted $15 billion in volume, spending $300,000 in transaction fees, only to receive $150,000 in tokens (a net loss of 50%). Users' real money was directly converted into the project team's profit. ●Characteristics of "Anti-Fraud": A simple and brutal form of "reverse pump-and-dump." While wash trading does require strict scrutiny, only reviewing it after token issuance is blatantly using the guise of airdrops to earn transaction fees. Furthermore, the token BP plummeted 68% in its first week, with users being quietly drained by endless wash trading. ●Long-Term Impact: The image of Chinese entrepreneurs has been completely tarnished. The Chinese region became the hardest hit, and the stereotype of "Chinese-led projects = anti-fraud" is deeply ingrained in the community, leading to an unprecedented crisis of trust for subsequent Chinese-led Web3 projects during their initial launch.

11. EdgeX: The Decline of Perp DEX

●The Anti-Airdrop Process: After the L2 crash, Perp DEX, which required real money to pay transaction fees, was once regarded by retail investors as the last haven for airdrops. Although Lighter set a good precedent, by the time edgeX TGE: old users spent hundreds of thousands of dollars in transaction fees to get airdrops worth less than a thousand dollars, while more than 80 new "insider trading" addresses with no interaction records raked in nearly $100 million in shares. Subsequently, on-chain investigators confirmed its connection to black market activities by market makers, and the official account immediately shut down comments and disappeared, leaving behind a mess. ● Characteristics of Anti-Fraud: Openly rigging insider trading, retail investors acting as data cows, project teams don't even bother to pretend. ● Far-reaching Impact: The EdgeX fiasco completely shattered the narrative of Perp DEX's volume manipulation, turning endorsements from top institutions into a euphemism for sophisticated exploitation. Retail investors were utterly desperate, and smart money accelerated its return to CEXs or native L1 exchanges.

12. Genius: The Last Straw That Broke the Loose for the Loose Group

●The Anti-Loose Process: Genius was seen as the last hope, but after the community frantically boosted trading volume, TGE received a reversal gift: immediately claiming the airdrop within 7 days would automatically burn 70% of the tokens, with a maximum of 30%; or choosing to lock up for one year to receive the full amount. Under the overwhelming public pressure, the project team then urgently launched a "refund" option - within 48 hours of TGE, users could choose to burn 100% of the airdrop amount in exchange for a refund of the fees charged by Genius.

●Characteristics of Anti-Profiteering: Users invest real money based on a premium of trust, only to be told at the last minute, "Either take a small amount and leave, or spend another year with the project team."

●Profound Impact: Genius's questionable actions completely demystified the "top-tier platform narrative." It was described by the community as "the last straw that broke the camel's back for profiteering enthusiasts."

●Characteristics of Anti-Profiteering: Users invest real money based on a premium of trust, only to be told at the last moment, "Either take a small amount and leave, or spend another year with the project team."

●Profound Impact: Genius's questionable actions completely demystified the "top-tier platform narrative." It was called "the last straw that broke the camel's back for profiteering

Conclusion: A Decisive Action to Get to the Root of the Problem

From HOP's witch hunt list to Blast's nested points system, and then to LayerZero's self-surrender and massacre... these twelve project teams have collectively written an absurd and cruel history of blood and tears for retail investors in the cryptocurrency world.

But the truth may be even more cruel: this is not only a premeditated harvest, but also a collective karma of speculation and greed.

But the truth may be even more cruel: this is not only a premeditated harvest, but also a collective karma of speculation and greed.

For a long time, the airdrop community only cared about "whether or not to issue tokens and how to distribute them," ignoring whether the products had genuine product-to-market value (PMF) or could generate sustainable revenue. Project teams precisely exploited this greed – you were after the airdrop, they were after your principal and transaction fees. Now, the airdrop bubble has burst, and countless people have been severely burned. While this is undoubtedly tragic, it's also a necessary, albeit painful, cleansing. The market has finally been forced to return to common sense: traffic attracted by airdrop expectations is ultimately a mirage; only products with genuine PMF are worth investing time and money in. This is the end of airdrops and the rebirth of Web3. Those projects that started with manipulation and opaque practices will ultimately be eliminated by users' votes with their feet; while those projects truly willing to build with the community and return to the essence of value will win more precious trust from the ruins. For those who enjoy admiring and embracing certain things, this was a painful lesson, but also a sobering turning point.

data.title: Solana, please match your speed.
data.description:
Author: Jito Labs CEO/Co-founder Lucas Bruder

Original Title: Solana's Napster Era Is Over

Compiled and edited by: BitpushNews

Solana is a masterpiece. But what's built on top of it doesn't deserve that title.

I'm not saying this lightly. My team has been working on Solana's infrastructure layer for four years. We've witnessed every stage of this chain's growth: crashes, recoveries, explosive growth in activity, and the maturation of the validator network. We've watched transaction volumes grow from a few thousand to millions, to numbers that make traditional exchanges uneasy. I believe in this chain more than anyone else.

But infrastructure isn't what people experience. People experience the applications.

Now, almost nothing on Solana is built for users who are already there and deserve a better experience, let alone the millions coming soon. How did we get here? This mirrors the trajectory of the internet. The first large-scale applications of the internet were pirated MP3s and pornography. I was six when Napster launched in 1999, and within two years it had 80 million users sharing pirated Metallica albums. Early internet engineers thought they were building the future of communication, but the first wave of users emerged for the most "degenerate" possibilities. This wasn't a failure of the internet, but rather proof that the "pipeline" was working. It just took a few more years before Amazon, Google, or the iPhone emerged on the same infrastructure. Solana has been in its Napster era. Its infrastructure is indeed world-class. The entire developer ecosystem revolves around "shortest-handling with the latest tokens": more token issuance platforms, more dashboards tracking memecoin price movements. All development targets the same set of wallets, and all optimizations are designed to help people get in and out as quickly as possible, not to help them trade better. Supporters have consistently argued that memecoin was crucial for developing and stress-testing Solana's engineering capabilities. They are right, but that argument belongs to the past. Dogecoin brought us here, but it's not the destination. Whether the ecosystem is ready or not, the Napster era is ending.

DEX vs. CEX Spot Volume Ratio (Source: Blockworks Analytics)

The world's largest fintech companies are racing to bring users on-chain.

Coinbase acquired Vector.fun last year as part of its massive acquisition spree, and now any Coinbase user can trade virtually any Solana token directly from the Coinbase App. Solana's DEX trading volume surpasses $1 trillion by 2025.

Robinhood is building its own blockchain to support tokenized stocks and perpetual futures, and you can already buy Solana tokens directly in your Robinhood wallet. These companies, with hundreds of millions of users combined, are no longer building standalone crypto products but weaving on-chain trading into the same experience people have when buying stocks and ETFs. The walls between CEXs (centralized exchanges) and DEXs (decentralized exchanges) are crumbling. And they chose Solana's infrastructure because it's the best. The next wave of 100 million users isn't a myth; they already have Coinbase and Robinhood installed on their phones. The question is, what will they find when they arrive? Currently, using many Solana apps feels like walking into a flea market. It's noisy everywhere, there are no labels, and you're unsure what's real and what's fake. There might be some good deals, but the environment doesn't give you any reason to trust it. In contrast, Robinhood and Coinbase have clean, uncluttered interfaces and intuitive controls; the whole experience makes you feel like someone anticipated your needs before you even had to. That's the difference. And that's exactly how the next wave of users will measure Solana.

Look at what Hyperliquid is doing: less emphasis on memes and a growing focus on real-world assets. Daily trading volume for oil perpetual contracts reaches $1.7 billion; S&P 500 contracts surpassed $100 million on their first day. The protocol generates over $600 million in annualized fees, and its token HYPE is up 50% this year, while most tokens have performed poorly. They've proven that when you build a serious trading experience, real trading volume follows. And they're doing it on their own L1, without Solana's advantage. We don't have technical problems; we have experience problems. Imagine what it's possible when someone develops at this level on top of the fastest infrastructure in the cryptocurrency space. The three dimensions of the experience gap: The first dimension is trust. People are used to apps that are intuitive and easy to use from the moment they open. Robinhood, Cash App, Apple Pay. You don't need to read manuals or decode interfaces. Everything works as expected, and when something goes wrong, the app tells you why in plain language. That's the baseline. Now think about what happens when these users first enter a Solana app. The experience immediately feels untrustworthy—not because of smart contracts or the escrow model, but because the interface doesn't look designed for them. The colors and the three vertical trend bars are practically a slot machine. No one explained what happened or why. They closed the tab within 30 seconds and went back to where they were. They weren't churned; they were never given a reason to stay. Trust is the feeling you get within the first three seconds of using a product—that feeling that "the developer respects your time and intelligence." Most products in this ecosystem are designed for people who already understand cryptocurrency. This works when all users are crypto natives; but it doesn't work when fintech companies are about to reach millions of people who don't even know what a mnemonic phrase is. The second dimension is craftsmanship. Craftsmanship is the result that developers truly "care" about. It's not just about speed of delivery, but about every detail from when you click to trade to settlement. How does the interface respond under network pressure? Does the loading status convey progress or just spin? Does the confirmation screen tell you the core information or bury it? Craftsmanship is a feeling: every interaction, every extreme situation, is anticipated by someone who understands your intentions. The Solana ecosystem has always moved too fast, so most teams have chosen to optimize delivery speed rather than this dedication. In the growth phase, that was the right choice; but the next phase requires different standards. The third dimension is durability. Most projects in the crypto space are optimized for the current cycle. Teams launch quickly, grab attention, follow the narrative, and leave when the narrative shifts. The entire ecosystem operates on a "hype and abandonment" rhythm. Users can feel this. They've been hurt too many times; they've developed an instinct. They can distinguish what's built for long-term existence from what's for short-term arbitrage. The truly important applications on Solana are those projects that teams see as a decade-long opportunity, not a cyclical trade. They are the applications that survive three, five, or ten years later, through bear markets and shifting landscapes. Traders know the difference. Nobody's going to spend time configuring a tool they think will disappear next year. In conclusion, Solana is at a turning point, and most in the ecosystem haven't realized it yet. The debate about infrastructure has settled. Solana is fast, cheap, and reliable. That's now the ticket, not a differentiating advantage. The next phase of competition isn't between chains, but between the applications built on top of them. And right now, the application layer doesn't match the ambition of its underlying infrastructure. The Napster era proved the chain viable. Millions of transactions daily, billions of dollars in volume, it withstood real pressure. That argument has won. What's next is harder: it requires building the next wave of applications that 100 million users truly want to use. The experiences that Coinbase and Robinhood are willing to proudly showcase to their customers. These experiences should make Hyperliquid's success on its own L1 look like a glimpse into the infinite possibilities of Solana, rather than a accusation that we failed to build anything good. The chain has grown. Now, it's time for everything on top of it to catch up.
